summary: Demis Hassabis discusses the tension between open science and AI safety, arguing that a slight slowdown in the field might be beneficial for addressing ethical and safety concerns. He emphasizes that AI's energy footprint will be vastly outweighed by its ability to solve critical scientific challenges like climate change and drug discovery, and urges the industry to focus on these pressing societal issues rather than frivolous entertainment.

principles.statement: Open science accelerates progress, but slowing down might be necessary to ensure safety.
principles.rationale: While fully sharing research makes the overall field advance faster, the rapid pace of AI development leaves very little time to sort out critical safety, philosophical, and ethical issues before reaching AGI.
principles.source_quote: if there's a full sharing of everything, then um uh progress overall is faster, right? ... but look, it may be a good thing that that it's not as fast as that because there's safety to worry about.

principles.statement: AI's benefits to science and climate will far outweigh its energy costs.
principles.rationale: AI is necessary to discover new materials, optimize national energy grids, and accelerate technologies like fusion, which will yield orders of magnitude more energy savings than the models consume.
principles.source_quote: these the the models that we're building like the climate models the weather models models for science they're going to far outweigh the benefits of that even in just the mara domain of energy... they're going to be orders of magnitude more uh uh improvement and saving than they use in terms of the energy costs

principles.statement: The AI industry should prioritize pressing societal challenges over frivolous entertainment.
principles.rationale: Society faces massive challenges in health, energy, and climate. AI has the potential to solve these, and focusing on these beneficial use cases will garner much stronger public support than focusing on video generation or entertainment.
principles.source_quote: I actually think AI we need AI to help us with some of the uh the pressing challenges facing society including in health and in in in energy and as you say I think actually society would be supportive of that.

principles.statement: Compute is essential for exploration, not just for training runs.
principles.rationale: Many new ideas in AI can only be validated if they are tested at a certain scale; without sufficient compute, researchers cannot explore these ideas to see if they hold up.
principles.source_quote: It's not just for the big training runs. It's also for exploring new ideas that you only know if they're good ideas if you do them at a certain scale. Otherwise, they don't hold.

frameworks.name: Targeted Data Gathering via Frontier Modeling
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining what expensive experimental data to collect for complex scientific problems (like drug discovery).
steps: Build proprietary models that push the absolute limit of what is currently possible.
Evaluate the overall system to identify exactly where it performs well and where it underperforms.
Deduce that areas of underperformance are caused by specific missing data.
Go out and gather or commission that specific experimental data to fill the gaps.
frameworks.source_quote: you see where does that system overall perform well where does it not perform well and then usually where it doesn't perform well that's because you need more data uh and then we go out and get that data... but we're the only ones who know what's the missing data because we have those um proprietary uh uh systems

mental_models.name: The One-Way Commons
mental_models.description: A dynamic where foundational research is published openly by a few actors, but others take it and build commercial products without contributing back to the shared knowledge pool at the same magnitude, leading to an unsustainable ecosystem.
mental_models.example: DeepMind and Google publishing Transformers, RL, and AlphaGo openly, which created the modern AI industry, while many companies using that research do not publish their own findings back to the commons.

mental_models.name: Bottleneck Whack-a-Mole
mental_models.description: In complex supply chains (like AI compute), resolving one constraint immediately exposes the next weakest link in the chain.
mental_models.example: Fixing general compute shortages only to realize that memory chips have now become the primary bottleneck for AI research.

heuristics: One year in AI feels like 10 years.
You have to ruthlessly prioritize between serving existing products and training new models.
Let the work speak for itself rather than announcing things prematurely.

signature_quotes.text: One year in AI feels like 10 years. So, I don't know if we want it to go any faster.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the pace of AI development and whether slowing down for safety is a bad thing.

signature_quotes.text: I actually think one of the only ways to tackle climate in today's fragmented political world is to come up with some new technologies, new materials, get fusion working... I think all of them require AI to accelerate.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing concerns about the energy usage of AI data centers.

signature_quotes.text: It's not just for the big training runs. It's also for exploring new ideas that you only know if they're good ideas if you do them at a certain scale.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why compute bottlenecks impact fundamental research, not just product deployment.

signature_quotes.text: I like our work to speak for itself. So, you can bet that we're working, you know, furiously behind the scenes to push forward.
signature_quotes.context: When asked why Isomorphic Labs hasn't made many public announcements yet.

anti_patterns.description: Taking from the open-source AI commons without contributing back.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is unsustainable over the long term if only a few organizations publish foundational work while others just build on it without reciprocating to the same magnitude.

anti_patterns.description: Focusing AI development primarily on frivolous entertainment.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the pressing challenges facing society (health, energy, climate) where AI could have orders of magnitude more impact and garner much stronger public support.

anti_patterns.description: Using quantum computers to simulate biology or disease.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Quantum computers are suited for modeling quantum systems (atoms, electrons, materials, chemistry), not large-scale emergent biological complexity like cells, which require traditional experimental data at scale.
